title: Safe Harbor de-identification
clauseText: Protected health information is de-identified only when all 18 listed identifiers of the individual and of relatives, employers, or household members are removed, and the covered entity has no actual knowledge that the remaining information could identify the individual.
exampleViolations: keeping any of the 18 identifiers in a dataset intended to be de-identified
releasing health records that still contain names, dates, or record numbers

title: Identifier: Names
clauseText: Names of the individual or of relatives, employers, or household members must be removed for de-identification.
exampleViolations: a patient name column alongside health data
next-of-kin or guarantor names in a medical record export

title: Identifier: Dates linked to an individual
clauseText: All elements of dates (except year) directly related to an individual, including birth date, admission date, discharge date, and date of death, must be removed; ages over 89 and dates indicating such age must be aggregated.
exampleViolations: storing exact date of birth or admission date with patient records
keeping a date of death linked to an identifiable person

title: Identifier: Medical record and health plan numbers
clauseText: Medical record numbers, health plan beneficiary numbers, and account numbers must be removed for de-identification.
exampleViolations: a medical record number (MRN) column in a dataset
health-plan beneficiary IDs stored alongside diagnoses

title: Identifier: Biometric and other unique identifiers
clauseText: Biometric identifiers including finger and voice prints, full-face photographs and comparable images, and any other unique identifying number, characteristic, or code must be removed.
exampleViolations: storing fingerprints, voiceprints, or full-face patient photos
retaining a device serial number that uniquely identifies a patient

title: Minimum necessary
clauseText: A covered entity must make reasonable efforts to limit protected health information to the minimum necessary to accomplish the intended purpose of the use, disclosure, or request.
exampleViolations: including diagnosis or treatment columns not required for the analysis
exporting an entire patient record when only aggregate fields are needed

title: Access control and encryption
clauseText: A covered entity must implement technical policies and procedures, including encryption and access controls, that allow access to electronic protected health information only to authorized persons or software.
exampleViolations: storing PHI such as diagnoses in plaintext
logging protected health information to unsecured logs
sending PHI to a third party without safeguards
